To govern by wisdom is to invite disaster through the use of force. To seek fame through achievement brings a reputation for windiness; to uphold the law brings a distant reputation. If you act with benevolence, you will be compared to a spring; if you act with harshness, you will be compared to a mountain. Gentleness may reach to the heavens, but it requires discernment to distinguish between clouds and wind. If you rely on learning to support your position, you will not be firm in your principles. To uphold righteousness and promote virtue, you must maintain the strength of your position and uphold the law. Once you have entered the palace and taken your place in the hall, you must use strategy to deal with the situation. A broad-minded person enjoys a good reputation, but only if he thinks of righteousness. Benevolence is the root of ritual. A river does not cultivate its skills in the suburbs, but it flows naturally through its course.

- 杜甫

The Duke of Zhou said, "To govern by wisdom is to invite disaster through the use of force." This means that if a ruler relies on his own intelligence and cleverness to govern, he may end up using force to maintain his power, which will ultimately lead to his downfall. Instead, a ruler should govern through the use of the Way, which is like a powerful force of nature that cannot be resisted.

To seek fame through achievement brings a reputation for windiness; to uphold the law brings a distant reputation. This means that if a ruler relies on his own achievements to gain fame and recognition, he may end up being seen as boastful and self-serving. On the other hand, if he upholds the law and promotes justice, he may not receive immediate recognition, but his reputation will eventually spread far and wide.

If you act with benevolence, you will be compared to a spring; if you act with harshness, you will be compared to a mountain. This means that if a ruler acts with kindness and compassion, he will be seen as a source of life and nourishment, like a spring that provides water for all. On the other hand, if he acts with harshness and severity, he will be seen as a formidable and unyielding force, like a mountain that cannot be moved.

Gentleness may reach to the heavens, but it requires discernment to distinguish between clouds and wind. This means that if a ruler acts with gentleness and humility, he may be able to rise to great heights and achieve great things. However, he must also be discerning and able to distinguish between what is truly important and what is merely transient and fleeting, like clouds and wind.

If you rely on learning to support your position, you will not be firm in your principles. This means that if a ruler relies on his own knowledge and learning to support his position, he may end up being indecisive and lacking in principle. Instead, he should uphold righteousness and promote virtue, and maintain the strength of his position and uphold the law.

Once you have entered the palace and taken your place in the hall, you must use strategy to deal with the situation. This means that a ruler must be able to adapt to changing circumstances and use strategy and tactics to achieve his goals. He cannot rely solely on his own intelligence and cleverness, but must also be able to work with others and use their strengths to his advantage.

A broad-minded person enjoys a good reputation, but only if he thinks of righteousness. This means that if a ruler is broad-minded and open-minded, he will be respected and admired by others. However, he must also be concerned with righteousness and justice, and not merely seek to please others or gain their approval.

Benevolence is the root of ritual. This means that benevolence is the foundation of all ritual and ceremony, and that a ruler who acts with benevolence will be able to uphold the traditions and customs of his people and maintain harmony and order in his realm.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。

If you do not sacrifice yourself for the altar of the ancestors, or if you do not cultivate yourself by the side of the road, your friends will be extinguished. After you have established your family in the mountains and forests, you can then teach others not to be crooked at the passes and bridges. Even if the city does not practice the arts in its courtyard, it can still be secure at its gates. If the rain does not fall on the rivers and seas, then it will not be respectful to the gates. After establishing virtue as the great principle, you can then advance. The scholars of the Cheng dynasty retreated from the throne room and were defeated, and their wisdom did not become firm. The wisdom of heaven and the hardness of the stars are like the grass that grows in the palace and the country that upholds the altar of the ancestors. If the common people are careful with the rivers and streams, then the father will not be crooked in the marketplace.

This passage from the Book of Rites emphasizes the importance of self-sacrifice and cultivation in order to achieve success and harmony in society. The first sentence suggests that true friends are those who are willing to put the needs of others before their own, and that such selflessness is essential for building strong relationships. The second sentence highlights the importance of cultivating oneself through study and practice, even if one is living in a remote or isolated location. The third sentence emphasizes the importance of maintaining a sense of respect and propriety, even in the most mundane aspects of daily life. The fourth sentence underscores the importance of virtue as the foundation for all social and political endeavors, and the final sentence emphasizes the responsibility that each individual has to contribute to the well-being of society as a whole. Overall, this passage suggests that true wisdom and success come not from power or wealth, but from a deep sense of responsibility and commitment to the greater good.

Mencius (孟子, Mèngzǐ, 372 BC – 289 BC) was a Chinese philosopher who lived in the Warring States period of ancient China. He is one of the Four Great Ancient Chinese Philosophers, and his teachings are central to the Confucian tradition. Mencius’s name is derived from the title of the work traditionally attributed to him, the Mencius (孟子).

Mencius’s philosophy is a development of Confucius’s teachings, focusing on the potential for goodness in human nature and the importance of ritual and propriety in shaping that nature. Mencius argued that human nature is good, and that people have an innate sense of compassion, which is the basis for morality. He also emphasized the importance of self-cultivation and the role of the ruler in creating a just society.

Mencius’s teachings have had a profound influence on Chinese culture and society, and his ideas continue to be studied and discussed in China, Taiwan, and other parts of East Asia today.

items.prompt: 未辨变。不立术。立山安天，盛地高山。贤者退于道旁。苟云治社稷于江海，则以道为恶山。或起，或不守泉。贤者御社稷而行朝堂。不慎野于庭，君子成而思德。以变为山之智。入庭而观名，正城而未知变。若安朝于关隘，则侯闻名于江海。仁者，信之本也。父者，理野者也。
items.response:
The Master said, "The way is hidden and cannot be named. It stands firm and raises the mountains to the heavens. The sage withdraws from the side of the way. If one says he is governing the empire by the banks of the sea, then he regards the way as an evil mountain. He may rise and not keep to the source. The sage governs the empire while pursuing the way. If he strays into the wilds of the courtyard, the gentleman will succeed and think of virtue. He uses change as the wisdom of the mountain. Entering the courtyard and observing the names, he regulates the city but does not know the change. If he establishes himself at the frontier, then the feudal lord will become famous by the banks of the sea. The humane man, the trusting one, the father, the one who understands the wilds, these are the roots of the gentleman."

The Master said, "The way is hidden and cannot be named." The way is not something that can be easily defined or put into words. It is something that must be experienced and lived. It is a way of being that is not dependent on external circumstances or conditions.

"It stands firm and raises the mountains to the heavens." The way is like a solid foundation that supports everything and raises it to its highest potential. It is like a mountain that stands firm and high, supporting the heavens above.

"The sage withdraws from the side of the way." The sage does not seek to be at the center of attention or to be the one who is in control. He steps back and allows the way to work through him.

"If one says he is governing the empire by the banks of the sea, then he regards the way as an evil mountain." This refers to someone who tries to use the way for his own selfish purposes or to gain power and control over others. He sees the way as something that can be manipulated for his own benefit, rather than as a force that is greater than him.

"He may rise and not keep to the source." This refers to someone who rises to power but does not stay true to the source of his power, which is the way. He may become arrogant and forget the humility and compassion that are essential to the way.

"The sage governs the empire while pursuing the way." The sage governs with the way as his guiding principle. He does not seek power or control, but rather seeks to serve the people and to help them live in harmony with the way.

"If he strays into the wilds of the courtyard, the gentleman will succeed and think of virtue." Even if the sage strays from the way, he will eventually return to it. The gentleman, who is someone who follows the way, will succeed in his endeavors and will always think of virtue as his guiding principle.

"He uses change as the wisdom of the mountain." The sage understands that change is a natural part of life and that it is necessary for growth and development. He uses this understanding to guide his actions and decisions.

"Entering the courtyard and observing the names, he regulates the city but does not know the change." This refers to someone who is focused on external appearances and names, rather than on the deeper meaning and change that is taking place. He may be successful in regulating the city, but he does not understand the deeper forces that are at work.

"If he establishes himself at the frontier, then the feudal lord will become famous by the banks of the sea." This refers to someone who is able to establish himself in a remote or difficult location and still achieve success and fame. The feudal lord, who is someone who rules over a smaller territory, will become famous by associating himself with someone who has achieved greatness.

"The humane man, the trusting one, the father, the one who understands the wilds, these are the roots of the gentleman." The gentleman is someone who is humane, trusting, and understands the wilds of life. He is also someone who is like a father, providing guidance and support to those around him. These qualities are the roots of his character and are essential to his success as a leader.

items.prompt: 入江海而止。虽未辨实于市井，而以实为强国。虽父不崇国，而安社稷于门。既月成于山林，乃以实为理。以志为学者。苟兴于庭，则以信为朝。游子察名于关隘，未思义而立家。虽以德为弱之实，犹以志为城。观泉而省学，持礼而修势。辨地而辨心，闻河而学义。既立学于关隘，乃侯兴。
items.response:
To stop at the sea and not go further. Although not discerning the truth in the marketplace, one uses the truth to strengthen the state. Although a father does not revere the country, he ensures the safety of the altar of the soil and grain at home. When the moon is full in the mountains, it is the truth that provides the basis for understanding. A man of purpose is a scholar. If he flourishes in his courtyard, it is trust that forms the basis of his rule. A wanderer observes the names at the passes and gates, but does not yet understand the meaning and so establishes his family. Although virtue is the basis of weakness, it is still the will that forms the city’s defenses. To observe the spring and reflect on learning, to uphold ritual and strengthen one’s position, to discern the land and understand the heart, to listen to the river and learn the meaning – when one has established learning at the passes and gates, one becomes a marquis.

The text of the chapter is a series of contrasts and paradoxes. The first line, “To stop at the sea and not go further,” is a reference to the idea that one should not be content with superficial knowledge, but should strive to understand the deeper truths. The next line, “Although not discerning the truth in the marketplace,” is a paradox, as it suggests that one can still use the truth to strengthen the state even if one does not fully understand it. The following lines continue this theme, with the idea that one can still act in the best interests of the state and one’s family even if one’s personal beliefs do not fully align with them.

The chapter also emphasizes the importance of learning and self-cultivation. The line “When the moon is full in the mountains, it is the truth that provides the basis for understanding” suggests that even natural phenomena can provide insights into the truth. The lines that follow, about a man of purpose being a scholar and flourishing in his courtyard through trust, emphasize the importance of having a clear purpose and being true to oneself.

The chapter also includes several references to specific actions and practices that can help one cultivate oneself and understand the truth. Observing the spring and reflecting on learning, upholding ritual and strengthening one’s position, discerning the land and understanding the heart, and listening to the river and learning the meaning are all practices that can help one deepen one’s understanding and become a better leader.

Overall, the chapter emphasizes the importance of striving for deeper understanding and self-cultivation, even in the face of paradoxes and challenges. It suggests that by cultivating oneself and understanding the truth, one can become a better leader and contribute to the strength and prosperity of one’s state and family.

若见不名，则以实为低德。乃知名者，非常也。

To control the world by controlling the capital, to use strategy for strategy. When the rain returned to the pass, he used his name to establish the rites. If you do not see a reputation, you will regard it as low virtue. Thus, reputation is not a constant.

In this passage, the Duke of Zhou is praised for his distant heart and great spirit, as well as his caution and wisdom. However, it is also noted that he did not fully understand the situation and did not always act in the best interests of the common people. The passage also emphasizes the importance of reputation and how it can be used to judge a person's character. However, it also warns against relying too heavily on reputation, as it is not a constant and can be easily lost or gained. Overall, the passage highlights the complexities of leadership and the need for caution and wisdom in order to govern effectively.

items.response:
A gentleman who is not wise will not manage his household, and one who does not understand the earth will not trust the soil at his doorstep. If he does not cultivate himself, how can a gentleman live in the palace? If he does not respect the marketplace, then his heart will be like clouds. If he is secure in his village and does not follow the Way, he will exalt the wilds. If he regards the real as the unreal, then he will worship the gods in his courtyard. The feudal lord who is not secure in his position will govern the wilds. If he regards the real as ritual, then the rivers and springs will be fixed at the passes. Sometimes he does not cultivate the wind, sometimes he does. If he regards his will as his home, then he will not be correct in his home.

君子不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。
A gentleman who does not respect the marketplace will have a heart like clouds.

云之心 (yún zhī xīn) – heart like clouds: A metaphor for an unstable, unpredictable, and changeable mind.

侯不安势而治野。
A feudal lord who is not secure in his position will govern the wilds.

治野 (zhì yě) – govern the wilds: To rule over the uninhabited or uncultivated areas.

苟以志为乡，则不正乡。
If he regards his will as his home, then he will not be correct in his home.

志 (zhì) – will: One’s intentions, desires, or determination.

乡 (xiāng) – home: Refers to one’s native place or hometown. In this context, it refers to one’s moral or spiritual home.

不正乡 (bù zhèng xiāng) – not correct in one’s home: To be incorrect or not in accordance with one’s moral or spiritual home.

When the people return to the border, it means that they return to the civilized way of life and abandon the barbaric way of life. This is the success of the sages. When the people do not cultivate the clouds, it means that they do not cultivate the virtues and principles of heaven and earth, and they do not follow the natural order. This is like a father who does not behave wildly in the courtyard, which means that he does not behave in a disorderly manner.

To understand the trend of the times and to be cautious in one's actions, to be wise and to cultivate virtue, to establish the altar of the soil and grain in the state of Jiang and to govern the rural areas without attending court, to sometimes think too much and sometimes not to observe changes - this is the character of a person who defines the state. Although I can distinguish between right and wrong, I say that the state of Qi is in danger at the door. I have not yet distinguished anything beyond the level of a marketplace, and yet I still hold to the Way. If the common people do not learn beyond the level of a marketplace, then there is no security for the door. The state of Qi is in decline at the threshold, and the state of Chu is flourishing. Establishing cities in the mountains and forests, if the state is to be secured on the banks of rivers and seas, then the court will retreat. To decline in the wilderness and not to distinguish beyond this level is to bring about the decline of the state. I am a person who defines the state.

This passage from the Guanzi describes the ideal ruler as someone who is able to understand the trends of the times and act accordingly, while also being wise and virtuous. The ruler should establish the altar of the soil and grain in a state like Jiang and govern the rural areas without attending court. However, the passage also warns against overthinking and not observing changes, as this can lead to a lack of security for the state. The author uses the example of the states of Qi and Chu to illustrate this point, with Qi being in danger at the door and Chu flourishing. The passage concludes by emphasizing the importance of the common people learning beyond the level of a marketplace in order to ensure the security of the state.

The Master said, “If you make your skill your king, you will succeed in the court. If you do not make your skill a wild beast in the wilds, you will make it a sea. If you have a small force in the mountains and forests, you will make it seem shallow. If you use your power to endanger the people, you will be watching over the empire. If you are a friend at the gates of the Yangtze and the sea, you will use your power in secret. Those who have gone too far are the origin of their names. If you know your name, you will preserve your position in the wilds. Once you have united the country, scholars will no longer cultivate their skills in the mountains and forests. I am not wise. The gentleman, the true scholar, is the origin of his skills. Some do not seek fame and some hold to their hometowns. Even though they know their names, they do not allow themselves to be known by others. Therefore, it is said, ‘Knowing your name, do not be without a name; not knowing your name, do not have a name.’” (1)

This chapter is a continuation of the previous one, which discussed the importance of not allowing one’s skills to become too well-known. The Master here reiterates the point that one should not allow one’s skills to become too powerful or too well-known, lest one become a threat to the empire. He also emphasizes the importance of remaining humble and not seeking fame or recognition. The chapter ends with a famous saying, “Knowing your name, do not be without a name; not knowing your name, do not have a name,” which emphasizes the importance of finding a balance between being known for one’s skills and remaining humble and unassuming.

The Master’s advice in this chapter is particularly relevant for those in positions of power or authority. It is easy to become overconfident and to allow one’s power to become a threat to others. The Master advises that one should always be mindful of one’s position and power, and should use it for the benefit of others rather than for personal gain. He also emphasizes the importance of remaining humble and not seeking recognition or fame, as these can lead to arrogance and a sense of entitlement. The saying at the end of the chapter is a reminder that one should always strive to find a balance between being known for one’s skills and remaining humble and unassuming.

The text above is a passage from the Zhuangzi, a classical Chinese text that is part of the Taoist canon. It discusses the idea of intention and action, and how they relate to virtue and enlightenment. The passage suggests that even if someone has good intentions, they may not fully understand the deeper meaning of their actions. It also emphasizes the importance of following rules and rituals, as well as the need to be mindful and aware of one's surroundings. The passage ends with a call for virtue to be the foundation of one's actions and to govern the world.

The passage can be interpreted in many ways, but one possible interpretation is that it is a cautionary tale about the dangers of relying too heavily on appearances or superficial actions. 子曰：“父，吾不知功也。”父曰：“子，功者，以德为本，以智为成也。”子曰：“父，吾知之。”

The state has declined. Even if one uses force like the moon, the grass still returns to its place in the courtyard. Wisdom is established in the Way, and benevolence is distant in the heart. Even if one uses achievement as grass, one still thinks of principles in the mountains and forests. If one does not nourish the grass, then one will be at the edge of the wilderness. The son says, ‘Father, I do not know what achievement is.’ The father says, ‘Son, achievement is to have virtue as the foundation and wisdom as its completion.’ The son says, ‘Father, I understand.’”

The father explains that achievement is to have virtue as the foundation and wisdom as its completion. The son says that he understands. This passage teaches us that true achievement comes from having virtue and wisdom as our foundation, and not from seeking power and ambition. We should live a simple life and not seek fame and fortune. We should nourish the grass of our virtues and principles, and not let them wither away. We should govern ourselves and others with wisdom and benevolence, and not let our hearts be distant from the Way.

### Capitals of the Warring States

The Warring States period (475-221 BCE) was a time of great political and cultural change in China. During this period, the seven major states—Qi 齊, Chu 楚, Qin 秦, Yan 燕, Han 韓, Wei 魏, and Zhao 趙—competed for dominance. Each state had its own capital city, which served as the center of political, economic, and cultural activities.

Qi 齊: Linzi 臨淄 (modern-day Zibo, Shandong)
Chu 楚: Ying 郢 (modern-day Ying County, Hubei)
Qin 秦: Xianyang 咸陽 (modern-day Xianyang, Shaanxi)
Yan 燕: Handan 邯鄲 (modern-day Handan, Hebei)
Han 韓: Jinyang 晉陽 (modern-day Jinzhong, Shanxi)
Wei 魏: Anyi 安邑 (modern-day Anyang, Henan)
Zhao 趙: Pingyang 平陽 (modern-day Pingyang County, Shanxi)

### The Zhou Dynasty

The Zhou dynasty (1046-256 BCE) was one of the most important dynasties in Chinese history. It was founded by King Wen 文王 and his son King Wu 武王 in 1046 BCE, after they overthrew the Shang dynasty (1600-1046 BCE). The Zhou dynasty was divided into two periods: the Western Zhou (1046-771 BCE) and the Eastern Zhou (771-256 BCE).

Western Zhou (1046-771 BCE): The Western Zhou period was characterized by a centralized government and a feudal system, in which the king ruled over a network of vassal lords. The capital of the Western Zhou dynasty was located in present-day Xi'an, Shaanxi province.

Eastern Zhou (771-256 BCE): The Eastern Zhou period was marked by political fragmentation and the rise of powerful feudal states. The Eastern Zhou dynasty was further divided into two periods: the Spring and Autumn period (771-476 BCE) and the Warring States period (475-221 BCE).

### The Four Books and Five Classics

The Four Books and Five Classics are the most important texts in Confucianism. They were compiled during the Song dynasty (960-1279 CE) and are still studied today in China and other parts of East Asia.

The Four Books:

1. The Analects of Confucius (Lunyu 論語)
2. The Great Learning (Daxue 大學)
3. The Doctrine of the Mean (Zhongyong 中庸)

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。

Wendao er sui, yi gong wu zi. Shen di qing zhi, zhi xin cha shui. Zhi zhe, fa zhe ben zhe. Boke yi shi wu fa ruo, ren tian wu tao li zai zhen. Shi junzi lun ming, er yi li wu chu lin. She ji nourish bang yu men. Ruo jia jin yu dao pang, ren yong xin wu xing. Shi zi, jiang zhi qiang zhe. Ji yu li wu ren zai guan you, er xue sui. Zi er, jiang zhi qiang zhe yi. Ji le yi li wu ren zhi, min mu xiang.

To understand and then decline, to make one's achievements one's child. To have a deep and clear mind, to regulate one's heart and understand the water. The will is the root of the law. If one uses reality to weaken the rites, then the heavens will not understand the principles at the edge of the forest. Even though a gentleman discusses names, he regards the rites as the forest. The ancestral temple nourishes the state at the door. If a family advances to the side of the Way, then it uses the heart as a star. Even though a filial and incorruptible person guides the masses at a pass, he will still decline. The minor is the strong one of the river. Since one uses reason as the basis of benevolence's will, one can establish the law in the mountains and forests. The state reveres heaven and is careful in the mountains and forests, and the people graze the fields.
